description: Legislation dealing with cybercrime, electronic commerce, data protection, and digital rights in India

sections.title: Penalty and compensation for damage to computer, computer system, etc.
sections.text: If any person without permission of the owner or any other person who is in charge of a computer, computer system or computer network, accesses or secures access to such computer, computer system or computer network or downloads, copies or extracts any data, or introduces or causes to be introduced any computer contaminant or computer virus, or damages or causes to be damaged any computer, he shall be liable to pay damages by way of compensation to the person affected.
sections.simplified: If someone hacks your computer, steals your data, introduces a virus, or damages your system without permission, they must pay you compensation. This covers all unauthorized access to computers and networks.

sections.title: Compensation for failure to protect data
sections.text: Where a body corporate, possessing, dealing or handling any sensitive personal data or information in a computer resource which it owns, controls or operates, is negligent in implementing and maintaining reasonable security practices and procedures and thereby causes wrongful loss or wrongful gain to any person, such body corporate shall be liable to pay damages by way of compensation.
sections.simplified: Companies that handle your personal data (banks, hospitals, apps) MUST protect it properly. If your data leaks due to their negligence, they are liable to compensate you.

sections.title: Tampering with computer source documents
sections.text: Whoever knowingly or intentionally conceals, destroys or alters or intentionally or knowingly causes another to conceal, destroy, or alter any computer source code used for a computer, computer programme, computer system or computer network, shall be punishable with imprisonment up to three years, or with fine which may extend up to two lakh rupees, or with both.
sections.simplified: Tampering with, destroying, or hiding computer source code is punishable with up to 3 years in jail and/or ₹2 lakh fine.
sections.punishment: Up to 3 years and/or ₹2 lakh fine

sections.title: Computer related offences (Hacking)
sections.text: If any person, dishonestly or fraudulently, does any act referred to in section 43, he shall be punishable with imprisonment for a term which may extend to three years or with fine which may extend to five lakh rupees or with both.
sections.simplified: Hacking (unauthorized computer access done dishonestly or fraudulently) is a criminal offence punishable with up to 3 years imprisonment and/or ₹5 lakh fine.
sections.punishment: Up to 3 years and/or ₹5 lakh fine

sections.title: Offensive messages through communication service (STRUCK DOWN)
sections.text: Section 66A was struck down by the Supreme Court in Shreya Singhal v. Union of India (2015) as unconstitutional for violating freedom of speech under Article 19(1)(a).
sections.simplified: Section 66A which criminalized 'offensive' online messages was STRUCK DOWN by the Supreme Court in 2015. You CANNOT be arrested for posting opinions online under this section. However, authorities sometimes still misuse it — if this happens to you, it is illegal.

sections.title: Punishment for dishonestly receiving stolen computer resource or communication device
sections.text: Whoever dishonestly receives or retains any stolen computer resource or communication device knowing or having reason to believe the same to be stolen computer resource or communication device, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years or with fine which may extend to one lakh rupees or with both.
sections.simplified: Receiving or keeping a stolen phone, laptop, or any communication device when you know it's stolen is punishable with up to 3 years jail and/or ₹1 lakh fine.
sections.punishment: Up to 3 years and/or ₹1 lakh fine

sections.title: Punishment for identity theft
sections.text: Whoever, fraudulently or dishonestly make use of the electronic signature, password or any other unique identification feature of any other person, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years and shall also be liable to fine which may extend to one lakh rupees.
sections.simplified: Using someone else's password, electronic signature, Aadhaar, or any digital identity fraudulently is identity theft, punishable with up to 3 years jail and ₹1 lakh fine.
sections.punishment: Up to 3 years and ₹1 lakh fine

sections.title: Punishment for cheating by personation using computer resource
sections.text: Whoever, by means of any communication device or computer resource cheats by personating, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years and shall also be liable to fine which may extend to one lakh rupees.
sections.simplified: Online impersonation (pretending to be someone else using technology to cheat) is punishable with up to 3 years jail and ₹1 lakh fine. This covers phishing, fake profiles, and online scams.
sections.punishment: Up to 3 years and ₹1 lakh fine

sections.title: Punishment for violation of privacy
sections.text: Whoever, intentionally or knowingly captures, publishes or transmits the image of a private area of any person without his or her consent, under circumstances violating the privacy of that person, shall be punished with imprisonment which may extend to three years or with fine not exceeding two lakh rupees, or with both.
sections.simplified: Taking, sharing, or publishing private/intimate images of someone without their consent (like hidden camera recordings, revenge porn) is punishable with up to 3 years jail and/or ₹2 lakh fine.
sections.punishment: Up to 3 years and/or ₹2 lakh fine

sections.title: Punishment for publishing or transmitting obscene material in electronic form
sections.text: Whoever publishes or transmits or causes to be published or transmitted in the electronic form, any material which is lascivious or appeals to the prurient interest or if its effect is such as to tend to deprave and corrupt persons, shall be punished on first conviction with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years and with fine which may extend to five lakh rupees.
sections.simplified: Publishing or sharing obscene content online is punishable with up to 3 years jail and ₹5 lakh fine for first offence. Repeat offence: up to 5 years jail and ₹10 lakh fine.
sections.punishment: First: up to 3 years and ₹5 lakh. Repeat: up to 5 years and ₹10 lakh

sections.title: Punishment for publishing or transmitting of material containing sexually explicit act
sections.text: Whoever publishes or transmits or causes to be published or transmitted in the electronic form any material which contains sexually explicit act or conduct shall be punished on first conviction with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to five years and with fine which may extend to ten lakh rupees.
sections.simplified: Sharing sexually explicit content online carries even heavier penalties: up to 5 years jail and ₹10 lakh fine (first offence). Repeat: up to 7 years and ₹10 lakh fine.
sections.punishment: First: up to 5 years and ₹10 lakh. Repeat: up to 7 years and ₹10 lakh

sections.title: Punishment for publishing or transmitting material depicting children in sexually explicit act
sections.text: Whoever publishes or transmits or causes to be published or transmitted material in any electronic form which depicts children engaged in sexually explicit act or conduct, shall be punished on first conviction with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to five years and with fine which may extend to ten lakh rupees.
sections.simplified: Child pornography (CSAM) is severely punished: up to 5 years jail and ₹10 lakh fine (first offence). Repeat: up to 7 years and ₹10 lakh fine. Even possessing, browsing, or downloading such material is a crime.
sections.punishment: First: up to 5 years and ₹10 lakh. Repeat: up to 7 years and ₹10 lakh

sections.title: Breach of confidentiality and privacy
sections.chapter: XII - Intermediaries Not to be Liable
sections.text: Save as otherwise provided in this Act or any other law for the time being in force, any person who, in pursuance of any of the powers conferred under this Act, rules or regulations made thereunder, has secured access to any electronic record, book, register, correspondence, information, document or other material without the consent of the person concerned discloses such electronic record, book, register, correspondence, information, document or other material to any other person, shall be punished with imprisonment for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine which may extend to one lakh rupees, or with both.
sections.simplified: Government officials or anyone who gains authorized access to your electronic data and then shares it without consent faces up to 2 years jail and/or ₹1 lakh fine.
sections.punishment: Up to 2 years and/or ₹1 lakh fine

sections.title: Punishment for disclosure of information in breach of lawful contract
sections.chapter: XII - Intermediaries Not to be Liable
sections.text: Save as otherwise provided in this Act, any person including an intermediary who, while providing services under the terms of lawful contract, has secured access to any material containing personal information about another person, with the intent to cause or knowing that he is likely to cause wrongful loss or wrongful gain discloses, without the consent of the person concerned, or in breach of a lawful contract, such material to any other person, shall be punished with imprisonment for a term which may extend to three years, or with fine which may extend to five lakh rupees, or with both.
sections.simplified: Companies, apps, or service providers who leak your personal data in violation of their contract can be jailed up to 3 years and/or fined ₹5 lakh.
sections.punishment: Up to 3 years and/or ₹5 lakh fine

sections.title: Exemption from liability of intermediary in certain cases
sections.chapter: XII - Intermediaries Not to be Liable
sections.text: An intermediary shall not be liable for any third party information, data, or communication link made available or hosted by him. However, the exemption shall not apply if the intermediary has conspired or abetted or aided or induced the commission of the unlawful act, or upon receiving actual knowledge or on being notified by the appropriate Government or its agency, the intermediary fails to expeditiously remove or disable access to that material.
sections.simplified: Platforms (social media, websites) are generally NOT liable for content posted by users. BUT they lose this protection if: they actively participate in illegal content, or they don't remove illegal content after being notified.
